description:
You are a performance engineer who has just been handed raw latency logs from two micro-services in a small distributed system.  
        Your job is to generate a concise summary file that SREs can ingest into their dashboards.

        1. Raw logs  
           • /home/user/services/auth/logs/request.log  
           • /home/user/services/inventory/logs/request.log  

           Each file already exists and is formatted exactly like this (one entry per line, **fields separated by a single space**):

                <epoch-milliseconds> <latency-in-milliseconds>

           Example line (do not copy):  
                1609459200123 17

        2. What you must produce  
           Create a new text file at the exact path  
                /home/user/perf_summary.log

           The file must contain **exactly two lines**, one per service, **with no header line**.  
           Each line must follow this strict pipe-delimited schema (no extra spaces):

                <service-name>|<avg-latency-ms with two decimals>|<p99-latency-ms>|<request-count>

           Requirements for every metric:
           • Average latency: arithmetic mean of all samples, rounded to two decimal places.  
           • 99th percentile latency: since the data set is tiny, use the maximum value in the log as the P99.  
           • Request count: total number of entries in the log.

           The lines must appear in ASCIIbetical order by service name (auth first, inventory second).

        3. Verification notes  
           Automated tests will:
           • Confirm that the file /home/user/perf_summary.log exists.  
           • Read its two lines and compare them byte-for-byte to the expected strings, including the newline at the end of each line.  
           • Reject any extra whitespace, headers, or additional lines.

        4. Allowed tooling  
           Use any standard GNU/Linux command-line utilities (e.g., cat, awk, grep, bc, paste, printf, etc.).  
           You should be able to complete the task with roughly 2–3 commands.

        Remember: only the summary file will be graded, not your on-screen output.  
        Once /home/user/perf_summary.log looks exactly as specified, you are done.
truth:
Pre-existing directories:
        • /home/user/services/auth/logs/
        • /home/user/services/inventory/logs/

        Pre-existing files and their exact contents:

        1. /home/user/services/auth/logs/request.log
                1609459201000 15
                1609459202000 10
                1609459203000 20
                1609459204000 5
                1609459205000 15

        2. /home/user/services/inventory/logs/request.log
                1609459201100 30
                1609459202100 25
                1609459203100 35
                1609459204100 40

        Expected summary output file (what the agent must create):

        Path: /home/user/perf_summary.log  
        Exact byte-for-byte contents (including the final newline character at the end of each line):

                auth|13.00|20|5
                inventory|32.50|40|4

        Each line must terminate with '\n'.

        The automated checker will open /home/user/perf_summary.log, read it as plain ASCII/UTF-8 text, and compare it to the two lines above.
